html:
Muhammad denies contagion; same hadith chain preserves the contradicting ruling Science Internal Contradictions Strange Theology Logic Strong Abu Dawud #3912
"The Messenger of Allah said: 'There is no infection...' A nomadic Arab asked: 'How is it that when a mangy camel comes among healthy camels it gives them mange?' He replied: 'Who infected the first one?'

[Same chain]: Abu Hurairah also transmitted — 'a diseased camel should not be brought with a healthy camel to drink water.' When confronted, Abu Hurairah said: 'I did not transmit it to you.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad denies contagion as superstition. When a Bedouin pushes back with the observable fact of mangy camels infecting healthy ones, Muhammad deflects with a counter-question. The same chain preserves a contradicting Prophetic ruling — isolate diseased camels from healthy ones — which Abu Hurairah denied transmitting when faced with the contradiction.

Why this is a problem

  1. The "no contagion" claim is empirically false, and the Bedouin's observation is correct. Muhammad's counter-question redirects to ultimate causation without addressing the Bedouin's point about proximate transmission.
  2. The isolation ruling contradicts the denial — if contagion doesn't exist, why isolate diseased animals? Both cannot be simultaneously rational, and the tradition preserved both without resolution.

Why it fails

Muhammad's response to the Bedouin's observation was a deflecting counter-question, not "yes they transmit it, but Allah is the ultimate cause." The isolation rule is then silent evidence that Muhammad accepted contagion-prudence in practice — making the no-contagion declaration inexplicable. Abu Hurairah denying his own transmission is the community's own recognition that the contradiction was visible. The Bedouin was right; the plain text says Muhammad denied it.

Muhammad predicted paternity by hair color and buttock width — the li'an procedure Women Internal Contradictions Sharia Law Logic Morality Science Strong Abu Dawud #2254
"The Prophet said: 'Look and see whether she gives birth to a child with eyes like antimony, wide buttocks and fat legs — if she did, Sharik bin Sahma' will be its father.' She then gave birth to a child of a similar description. The Prophet said: 'If it were not for what has already been stated in Allah's book, I would have dealt severely with her.'"

What the hadith says

Hilal accused his wife of adultery. When he could not produce four witnesses, Q 24:6-9 was revealed establishing the li'an (mutual-cursing) procedure. Muhammad then predicted paternity by physical features — wide buttocks and dark colouring would indicate the alleged adulterer. The child matched the predicted description.

Why this is a problem

  1. Paternity by hair colour and buttock width is empirically wrong. Features are polygenic; morphology cannot reliably identify biological parentage. Muhammad's confident prediction uses folk genetics that modern science has refuted.
  2. Q 24:6-9 was revealed as a personal exemption to a specific complaint — fitting a pattern of revelation arriving to solve individual problems (Q 33:37, Q 66:1-5). The cumulative pattern suggests revelation functioned as case-law generated by specific situations.

Why it fails

The "prophetic miracle" framing requires the folk-genetic theory to be correct, which it is not. The racial-features prediction — dark skin, curly hair, wide buttocks — tracks Arabic descriptions of East African ancestry and is preserved across multiple chains. DNA testing supplements the canonical procedure rather than replacing it, leaving operative a system whose foundational case-law turns on a false theory of physical paternity.

Muhammad wished his Companions had killed the apostate he just pardoned Prophetic Character Violence Apostasy & Blasphemy Governance Moral Problems Strong Abu Dawud #2684
"He turned to his Companions and said: 'Is not there any intelligent man among you who would stand to this (man) when he saw me desisting from receiving the oath of allegiance, and kill him?' They replied: 'We do not know what lies in your heart; did you not give us a hint with your eye?' He said: 'It is not proper for a Prophet to have a treacherous eye.'"

What the hadith says

At the Conquest of Mecca, Muhammad grudgingly pardoned Abdullah ibn Abi Sarh — an apostate scribe on his execution list — after three silent refusals and Uthman's intercession. Afterward, Muhammad expressed disappointment that no Companion had taken the silent pauses as a cue to kill him. When Companions said they were waiting for an eye-signal, Muhammad replied: "It is not proper for a Prophet to have a treacherous eye."

Why this is a problem

  1. Muhammad expressed disappointment that a man he just pardoned had not been killed. The grant of pardon did not dissolve the wish — it was merely constrained by a prophet-specific restriction.
  2. "Not proper for a Prophet" is prophet-specific, not a universal prohibition. The construction ma yanbaghi li-nabiyyin implies ordinary Muslim leaders are not necessarily bound by the same limit. And calling the killer the "intelligent" Companion shaped how the tradition conceptualized apostate-killing.

Why it fails

The hadith preserves Muhammad expressing regret post-pardon that the killing had not occurred — the principled restriction was a self-imposed limit, not a moral preference. The reformist universalisation of the no-treacherous-eye principle requires reading a prophet-specific construction as a general rule. The "intelligent man" framing remains in the canonical record, unretracted: the Companion who would have killed an apostate during a silent pardon was the intelligent one.

Change evil with your hand, then tongue, then heart — the hierarchy that underwrites vigilantism Governance Violence Moral Problems Internal Contradictions Theology Strong Abu Dawud #1141
"He who observes an evil deed should change it with his hand if he can do so; if he cannot, then with his tongue; if he cannot, then with his heart, and that is the weakest degree of faith."

What the hadith says

Muhammad establishes a three-tier hierarchy: physical intervention (hand) is the highest-faith response; verbal rebuke (tongue) is second; internal disapproval (heart) is explicitly ranked as "the weakest degree of faith."

Why this is a problem

  1. It elevates unilateral physical intervention as the highest religious act. A faithful Muslim capable of physical action who declines is choosing a weaker form of faith. Saudi Arabia's CPVPV, Iran's Gasht-e Ershad, the Taliban's vice ministry, and ISIS's hisbah units all cite this hadith — these are applications, not misreadings.
  2. "Weakest degree of faith" creates systematic pressure toward escalation. Heart-only disapproval is canonically labelled ad'af — the doctrine structurally incentivises confrontation over restraint.

Why it fails

The "authorised authority only" restriction is not in the hadith text. The Marwan narrative that frames the hadith shows an anonymous individual rebuking a governor — without requiring formal office. Modern Islamic states that implemented morality policing cite exactly this hadith as their warrant. The reformist narrowing is welcome moral progress; it argues against the plain text, not with it.

Gabriel skipped a visit because a puppy was under Muhammad's bed; Muhammad ordered all dogs killed Strange / Obscure Prophetic Character Cosmology Theology Animals Strong Abu Dawud #4158
"Gabriel promised to visit me last night, but he did not... It occurred to him that there was a pup under his bed. He ordered it removed... Gabriel said: 'We do not enter a house which contains a dog or a picture.' When the morning came, the Prophet ordered to kill dogs."

What the hadith says

Gabriel failed to keep a promised visit because a hidden puppy was under Muhammad's bed. After Gabriel explained the purity-protocol, Muhammad's next-morning response was to order dogs killed across Medina, with an exception for dogs guarding large orchards.

Why this is a problem

  1. Divine revelation was suspended by a domestic animal Muhammad didn't know was there. The canonical doctrine of reliable Quranic transmission requires Gabriel as a dependable channel. This hadith shows revelation contingent on physical-domestic conditions beyond the Prophet's awareness.
  2. A single hidden puppy triggered a city-wide dog-elimination order. The tradition's compassion-for-animals teachings coexist, unresolved, with this canonical mass-killing precedent that became the basis for 1,400 years of prohibition on pet dogs.

Why it fails

The stated reason for the kill-order is Gabriel's purity-protocol — the "stray overpopulation" hypothesis is modern rationalization. Classical commentary (al-Nawawi, Ibn Hajar) accepted the broad kill-order as canonical. Fourteen centuries of Islamic jurisprudential consensus prohibiting pet dogs rests on this text. The reformist narrowing is not what the canonical text delivers.

Muhammad ratified a Christian convert's tale: hairy beast Jassasa, chained Dajjal in a monastery Eschatology Strange / Obscure Prophetic Character Pre-Islamic Borrowings Cosmology Strong Abu Dawud #4327
"A man on an island found a woman trailing her hair. She said: 'I am the Jassasa.' He came to a monastery and found a man chained in iron collars who asked about the palm-trees of Baisan and the spring of Zughar... Muhammad: 'Tamim al-Dari, a Christian, came and accepted Islam, and told me something which agrees with what I was telling you about the Dajjal.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad delayed prayer to announce that a recent Christian convert's sailing story confirmed his own Dajjal teachings. Tamim al-Dari and companions found a hairy female beast called the Jassasa and a chained Antichrist in a monastery interrogating them about Levantine geography.

Why this is a problem

  1. The sourcing flow is: convert's pre-Islamic story → Muhammad ratifies, not Prophet teaches → convert confirms. Muhammad says Tamim's story "agrees with" what he was already teaching — meaning independent sources converged. One source is the convert's Aramaic-Christian background.
  2. The Jassasa and the chained island Antichrist are late-antique Christian-apocalyptic figures with clear antecedents in Syriac literature. The details that betray the borrowing are precisely the details Muhammad ratified on the pulpit.

Why it fails

The "independent confirmation" framing is exactly what the hadith's grammar undermines: two sources converging is parallel tradition, not divine confirmation. The convert's Lakhmid-Christian background is the obvious source for the Syriac-apocalyptic details. A canonical eschatology whose Antichrist doctrine was ratified from a Christian sailor's pre-Islamic story is not a self-contained divine revelation.

"Where is Allah?" "In the heaven" — two questions certify a slave girl's belief and win her freedom Theology Slavery & Captives Women Prophetic Character Internal Contradictions Magic / Occult Strong Abu Dawud #931
"He asked her: Where is Allah? She said: In the heaven. He said: Who am I? She replied: You are the Messenger of Allah. He said: Set her free, for she is a believer."

[Same hadith]: "There was a prophet who drew lines; so if the line of anyone tallies with this line, that might come true."

What the hadith says

Muhammad asks a slave girl two questions; her correct answers certify her as a believer and win her freedom. In the same conversation, he partially endorses line-divination: a prior prophet drew lines and they sometimes came true.

Why this is a problem

  1. "Where is Allah — In the heaven" is the canonical proof-text for a millennium of Sunni dispute on divine location. Athari/Salafi scholars cite this for Allah's literal spatial aboveness; Ash'aris read it figuratively. The dispute has not been resolved by the text itself.
  2. Muhammad partially endorses geomancy while the same conversation condemns soothsayers — condemning the practitioner and licensing the technique in one exchange.

Why it fails

A single hadith that has sustained a millennium of unresolved intra-Sunni dispute over God's location is not a hadith that answered its central question clearly. The "historically particular" geomancy defense concedes the door was opened across fourteen centuries of Muslim practice. The text entangles Allah's location, a slave girl's manumission, and a licensed divination technique in one canonical record that resists clean theological sorting.

Muhammad made Safiyyah's own emancipation her marriage dowry Slavery & Captives Sexual Issues Prophetic Character Moral Problems Strong Abu Dawud #2054
"Anyone who sets his slave girl free and then marries her will have a double reward." (#2054)
"The Prophet manumitted Safiyya and made her manumission her dower." (#2055)

What the hadith says

The first hadith promises double reward for freeing a concubine and marrying her. The next records Muhammad executing this pattern with Safiyyah — a Jewish noblewoman captured at Khaybar, whose father and husband were killed the same day. Muhammad freed her and made her freedom the bridal payment.

Why this is a problem

  1. Ending an injustice you are imposing is not a wedding present. Standard mahr is wealth the husband transfers to the wife. Here Muhammad "gave" Safiyyah her freedom from a captivity he controlled — the gift is the removal of his own wrong.
  2. Consent is structurally impossible. Safiyyah had seen her husband killed hours earlier. Refusing the proposal meant remaining enslaved. A proposal whose only alternative is continued captivity is coercion. The case became a regularized legal template in classical jurisprudence.

Why it fails

The same person was the cause of the brutality and the rescuer from it — a role overlap no modern moral framework treats as consent-establishing. Elevating one woman from war-booty to wife-status leaves the war-booty framework fully operational for every other Khaybar woman. Her post-marriage outcomes do not address the consent question on the day of the marriage. The "freedom as mahr" device is morally incoherent: the man who imposed the captivity removes it as a gift.

Iron rings are "the adornment of Hell" — but Muhammad's own ring was iron polished with silver Ritual Absurdities Hell Contradiction Strange / Obscure Strong Abu Dawud #4224
"The Prophet said: 'What is it that I see you wearing the adornment of the inhabitants of Hell?' So he threw it away [the iron ring]." (#4224)
"The signet-ring of the Prophet was of iron polished with silver." (#4225)

What the hadith says

Muhammad tells a man that his iron ring is "the adornment of Hell-dwellers." The very next preserved hadith records that Muhammad's own signet-ring was made of iron polished with silver.

Why this is a problem

  1. The two adjacent hadiths flatly contradict. If iron rings are hellfire adornment, Muhammad's iron-core ring is hellfire adornment. Either the rule doesn't apply to him, or he violated his own ruling.
  2. Abu Dawud placed #4225 immediately after #4224 without editorial reconciliation, leaving the contradiction visible and unresolved in the canonical record.

Why it fails

The silver-overlay reading is classically contested — Arabic #4225 reads as iron with silver worked upon it, not iron covered by silver. If a silver surface over an iron core satisfies the rule, the distinction is arbitrary enough to render the prohibition meaningless. The deeper problem is structural: a religion's rules on permitted materials should not require post-hoc reconciliation between adjacent canonical chains. The reconciliation work is the evidence that these are 7th-century cultural conventions preserved and dressed as eternal moral law.

Kill the active and passive partner — the death sentence for same-sex acts Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud #4462
"Allah's Messenger said: 'Whoever of you find doing the action of the people of Lut, kill the one who does it and the one to whom it is done.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad prescribed death for both partners in a male homosexual act — no trial requirement specified, no distinction between coerced and consensual, no exemption for the passive or coerced party.

Why this is a problem

  1. Sahih al-Bukhari contains no equivalent hadith — Islam's most authoritative collection did not include it. The ruling appears in Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah, yet classical law followed the lesser collections over the Bukhari silence.
  2. "The one to whom it is done" includes the coerced victim — the command kills both parties, which in historical context frequently means executing the younger or coerced partner. Six Muslim-majority countries still impose the death penalty for same-sex acts, citing this jurisprudence.

Why it fails

The "practically inoperative" defense does not explain six active jurisdictions. The chain-grading argument fails when all four Sunni law schools endorsed the death penalty for same-sex acts — a consensus that cannot rest solely on a weak chain. The reformist work to narrow or retire this ruling requires arguing against the classical tradition's virtually unanimous reception. The real-world harm is ongoing and cites this text as its authority.

"Whoever changes his religion, execute him" — the apostasy death penalty Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #4351
"Whoever changes his religion, execute him."

"The blood of a Muslim man... is not permissible except in one of three cases: a married adulterer, a soul for a soul, and one who leaves his religion and separates from the Jama'ah."

What the hadith says

The command is general — anyone who leaves Islam is to be killed. The second hadith lists apostasy as one of three circumstances that make a Muslim's blood permissible. Classical Sunni jurisprudence across all four schools treated this as operative law.

Why this is a problem

  1. It makes Islamic belief involuntary from conversion onward. A person can enter Islam freely but may not leave on pain of death — the structure of a closed system, not a truth claim confident enough to allow reassessment.
  2. It directly contradicts Q 2:256 — "there is no compulsion in religion." Both cannot govern practice simultaneously. Classical jurisprudence restricted Q 2:256 to initial adoption, not departure — a restriction the verse does not state.

Why it fails

The restrictive reading is modern; the classical consensus treated apostasy itself as capital without requiring additional acts of war. Contemporary jurisdictions enforcing apostasy penalties apply them to private belief change — how the classical law historically operated. The tension with Q 2:256 is real and unresolvable: "no compulsion" and "leaving Islam is punishable by death" cannot both be operative. The classical solution was to limit Q 2:256 — a limit modern apologists quietly abandon while still citing the verse as proof of Islamic tolerance.

Aisha's consummation at nine — the swing, the preparation, the handover Prophetic Character Women Strong Abu Dawud #4933
"Umm Ruman came to me when I was on a swing... They took me, and prepared me, and adorned me. Then I was brought to the Messenger of Allah, and he consummated the marriage with me when I was nine years old." (Aisha)

What the hadith says

Aisha's own first-person account in four parallel Abu Dawud narrations — being taken off a swing, bathed, dressed, and delivered to Muhammad for consummation at age nine. One variant adds "my hair only came down to my ears," a marker of pre-pubertal physiology.

Why this is a problem

  1. Aisha is the eyewitness narrator. The revisionist age-redating requires rejecting a sahih-chain hadith narrated by Aisha herself, in the first person, across Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah. Rejecting her testimony undermines the hadith-science framework that certifies the entire Sunni corpus.
  2. Q 33:21 presents Muhammad as the pattern to imitate. Child marriage remains legal in several Muslim-majority countries partly on this precedent. The modeling claim is not theoretical.

Why it fails

The preservation of Aisha's voice is precisely what makes the revisionist redating impossible — she is the eyewitness. Her testimony cannot be overturned without repudiating the hadith-science methodology Islamic scholarship uses to certify everything else. The "culturally normal" defense concedes the ethics are historically contingent — which is exactly the problem with treating this as a prophetic precedent binding for all times. A moral exemplar whose behavior requires "it was normal then" is not functioning as a universal model.
